name: Kuo, Jonny
recent_1: Gonçalves, R. C., Goodridge, C. M., Kuo, J., Lenné, M. G., & Merat, N. (2024). Using driver monitoring to estimate readiness in automation: a conceptual model based on simulator experimental data. Cognition, Technology & Work, 26(4), 569-584. DOI: 10.1007/s10111-024-00777-3
recent_1_synopsis: Proposes a conceptual model for estimating driver readiness to resume control in conditionally automated driving, grounded in driver-monitoring-system (DSM) signals and simulator experimental data. The work links observable indices of driver state to a methodology for defining readiness thresholds, bridging human factors theory and the design of practical DSM alerting logic.
recent_2: Cai, A. W. T., Manousakis, J. E., Singh, B., Francis-Pester, E., Kuo, J., Jeppe, K. J., Rajaratnam, S. M. W., Lenné, M. G., Howard, M. E., & Anderson, C. (2024). Subjective awareness of sleepiness while driving in younger and older adults. Journal of Sleep Research, 33(1), e13933. DOI: 10.1111/jsr.13933
recent_2_synopsis: Examines how accurately younger and older drivers perceive their own sleepiness during driving, comparing subjective self-ratings with objective drowsiness measures. The study informs drowsy-driving countermeasures by characterizing age-related differences in metacognitive awareness of impairment, a key issue for self-regulation and the timing of monitoring-system interventions.
recent_3: Yang, S., Wilson, K. M., Roady, T., Kuo, J., & Lenné, M. G. (2022). Beyond gaze fixation: Modeling peripheral vision in relation to speed, Tesla Autopilot, cognitive load, and age in highway driving. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 171, 106670. DOI: 10.1016/j.aap.2022.106670
recent_3_synopsis: Extends driver attention modeling beyond foveal gaze fixation to incorporate peripheral vision, analyzing how speed, use of Tesla Autopilot, cognitive load, and driver age shape visual scanning on highways. The paper advances naturalistic and instrumented-vehicle methods for assessing situation awareness and distraction during partially automated driving.
seminal_1: Wilson, K. M., Yang, S., Roady, T., Kuo, J., & Lenné, M. G. (2020). Driver trust & mode confusion in an on-road study of level-2 automated vehicle technology. Safety Science, 130, 104845. DOI: 10.1016/j.ssci.2020.104845
seminal_1_synopsis: An on-road study of how drivers form trust in and experience mode confusion with production Level-2 automated vehicle technology. With roughly 76 Crossref citations (~114 on Google Scholar), it is one of Kuo's most influential works because it provides rare real-world (rather than simulator) evidence on trust calibration and mode awareness, foundational concerns for safe deployment of partial automation.
seminal_2: Louw, T., Kuo, J., Romano, R., Radhakrishnan, V., Lenné, M. G., & Merat, N. (2019). Engaging in NDRTs affects drivers' responses and glance patterns after silent automation failures. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 62, 870-882. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2019.03.020
seminal_2_synopsis: Demonstrates how engagement in non-driving-related tasks (NDRTs) degrades drivers' takeover responses and alters glance behavior following silent (un-alerted) automation failures. With roughly 72 Crossref citations (~107 on Google Scholar), it is foundational for the takeover-readiness and driver-distraction literature in automated driving, motivating much subsequent driver-monitoring research.
expertise_overview: Kuo's research centers on driver state monitoring, distraction, and fatigue/drowsiness in both manual and partially automated (Level 2/3) driving. He specializes in physiological and behavioral sensing methods, including eye-gaze and glance-pattern analysis, peripheral-vision modeling, ECG-based cognitive-workload detection, and in-vehicle camera-based monitoring systems. A recurring theme is takeover performance and readiness estimation when drivers engage in non-driving-related tasks, along with trust calibration and mode confusion with vehicle automation. His methods span driving simulators, instrumented on-road and naturalistic driving studies, and applied evaluation of commercial driver-monitoring technology (e.g., European NCAP DSM protocols, heavy-vehicle fleets). He works at the intersection of academic human factors and industry deployment, translating attention and impairment research into practical monitoring-system design and alerting thresholds.
notes: Identification confidence: HIGH. All six pre-verified Human Factors anchor papers confirm the driving/transport human factors researcher at Seeing Machines (Senior Human Factors Scientist) with a PhD from Monash University (Monash University Accident Research Centre), Melbourne, Australia; consistently co-authoring with Michael G. Lenné, Shiyan Yang, and Kyle M. Wilson (Seeing Machines) and with Natasha Merat / Tyron Louw (Leeds). Disambiguated from other 'Jonny/Jonathan Kuo' individuals via this collaborator network and topic. Sources: Crossref API (titles, authors, citation counts, metadata); Google Scholar profile (user=33vdJBUAAAAJ; ~1,112 total citations, h-index 18); ResearchGate; Seeing Machines corporate pages and DDI 2024 program. All six cited DOIs were verified to resolve via the Crossref API with matching titles and Kuo in the author list. Citation counts are Crossref 'is-referenced-by-count' values retrieved 2026-06-17 (Google Scholar counts noted in seminal synopses are higher, as is typical). No unverifiable citations; no invented DOIs.
